aliases: スフレチーズケーキ (Sufure Chiizkeki)

japanese: スフレチーズケーキ (Sufure Chiizkeki)

food_subgroup: Light, airy baked soufflé-style cheesecake made from cream cheese, beaten egg whites, sugar, flour, and milk; water-bath baked

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Japanese soufflé-style cheesecake. Closest parent is a generic baked cheesecake concept. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Japanese soufflé-style baked cheesecake (cream cheese, beaten egg whites, sugar, flour, milk, water-bath baked)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CQF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for baked cheesecake; no exact code exists for the Japanese soufflé variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Japanese soufflé cheesecake as a specific food entity.

preparation_method: egg whites beaten to stiff peaks and folded in

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of Japanese cheesecake weighs approximately 80g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold at Japanese bakeries such as Uncle Tetsu in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Cheesecake (component-based estimate from cream cheese, whole egg, sugar, cake flour, whole milk)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Japanese soufflé-style cheesecake. Values are derived from USDA SR Legacy component data for cream cheese (FDC ID 1017), whole egg (FDC ID 748967), granulated sugar (FDC ID 1151), cake flour (FDC ID 20081), and whole milk (FDC ID 746782), weighted to a representative Japanese cheesecake formulation (approx. 35% cream cheese, 20% egg, 20% sugar, 10% flour, 10% milk, 5% butter by weight). The resulting macros and micronutrients are consistent with published nutritional analyses of Japanese-style cheesecake. Significantly lighter fat profile (~15g/100g) compared to New York cheesecake (~25–35g/100g) due to the beaten egg white technique.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate: baked cheesecake with cream cheese, sugar, and egg; moderate GI driven by sucrose and milk solids (~55–60)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese soufflé cheesecake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: baked cheesecake with cream cheese and sucrose base (GI ~55–60 per University of Sydney GI Database). The fat content (15g/100g) moderates glucose absorption, supporting a medium GI classification. Glycemic load per 80g serving = (58 × 21.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.3, classified as moderate GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 255mg/100g from cream cheese natural salt content
hypertension.guidance: An 80g serving delivers approximately 204mg sodium; while low for a dessert, individuals with hypertension should limit to one slice and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load of 12.3 per 80g serving; fat content buffers glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: The moderate GL of 12.3 per serving and fat-buffered GI of 58 make a single 80g slice an acceptable occasional dessert for people with Type 2 Diabetes, but portion control is essential given 18g sugar per 100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.5g/100g and cholesterol 135mg/100g from cream cheese and egg yolks
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: An 80g serving provides 6.8g saturated fat (34% of a 20g daily limit) and 108mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional dessert and limit frequency to avoid cumulative saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Cheesecake (スフレチーズケーキ, Cotton Cheesecake) is a globally celebrated dessert that became a phenomenon in the 2010s, with queues stretching around the block outside Uncle Tetsu's Japanese Cheesecake in Singapore. It represents a uniquely Japanese interpretation of the Western cheesecake: infinitely lighter, more delicate, and barely sweet compared to the dense, rich New York cheesecake. The technique is a hybrid of soufflé and cheesecake: cream cheese, egg yolks, cake flour, and milk are combined into a smooth base, while egg whites are beaten separately to stiff peaks and carefully folded in. The cake is baked in a water bath (bain-marie) at low temperature to prevent cracking and ensure even, gentle cooking. The result is a cloud-like, jiggly, barely set cake that quivers when touched — the golden crust is thin and delicate, the interior silky and slightly tangy from the cream cheese, light as air. Nutritionally, it is significantly lighter than New York cheesecake: approximately 15g fat per 100g versus 25–35g for NY style, and 268 kcal per 100g. The beaten egg white technique reduces fat density while maintaining structure. Per 80g serving: approximately 214 kcal, 6g protein, 12g fat, 21.2g carbohydrate, 14.4g sugar, 204mg sodium. Moderate GI (~58) and moderate glycemic load (12.3 per serving). Suitable as an occasional dessert for most people including those managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed in single-slice portions. Available at Uncle Tetsu, Pablo, and many Singapore Japanese bakeries and cafés.
common_user_queries: Japanese cheesecake vs NY cheesecake calories

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Japanese soufflé-style cheesecake. Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy component data for cream cheese, whole egg, sugar, cake flour, and whole milk, weighted to a representative formulation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest baked cheesecake entry. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the component-based derivation rather than a direct food analysis entry.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this component-derived Japanese cheesecake formulation is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; individual component fatty acid data could be summed but would introduce additional estimation error beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific baked formulation is not reported as a single value in the USDA SR Legacy component sources consulted. All other nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy component data (cream cheese FDC ID 1017, whole egg FDC ID 748967, granulated sugar FDC ID 1151, cake flour FDC ID 20081, whole milk FDC ID 746782) and are consistent with the input record's verified values. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (closest match: baked cheesecake with cream cheese and sucrose base). The input record cited the University of Sydney GI Database as source with confidence 0.9; this is preserved. Halal status assumes no lard or pork-derived gelatin is used, consistent with standard Japanese bakery practice in Singapore. The food is a Japanese dish; mandatory Japanese local-language name (スフレチーズケーキ) is present. Chinese, Malay, and Tamil names are also provided given the Singapore context of this food's primary market.
